description: Machine-readable metadata for the experimental executable corpus used by the Hub batch testing workflow.

query_defaults.auto_triage_query: Analyze this executable at the program level. Identify the main behaviors, important functions, likely obfuscation or packing, and which available tools were most informative.
query_defaults.followup_query: Using the prior triage context, summarize what this sample does, explain the most relevant functions, and note any naming or type suggestions that would help future reverse-engineering.

expected_evidence: Recovered for/while/do-while loop structure
Call graph linking main to compute_sum, classify_value, and accumulate
No unsupported obfuscation or packing claim
acceptance_targets: Explain the core loop structure accurately
Name the key functions and their roles
Treat the sample as a baseline decompilation case rather than an obfuscated binary
samples.default_query: Analyze this executable and explain the core loop structure, key functions, and why it should be treated as a baseline decompilation sample.

tasks.name: Default Analysis
tasks.query: Analyze this executable and explain the core loop structure, key functions, and why it should be treated as a baseline decompilation sample.
expected_evidence: Recovered for/while/do-while loop structure
Call graph linking main to compute_sum, classify_value, and accumulate
No unsupported obfuscation or packing claim
acceptance_targets: Explain the core loop structure accurately
Name the key functions and their roles
Treat the sample as a baseline decompilation case rather than an obfuscated binary

primary_techniques: opcode dispatcher with live and dead entries
expected_evidence: Actual behavior identified as decoding a small script, rendering a 12x8 in-memory snapshot, summarizing it, and writing maintenance_snapshot_report.txt
Threatening names or strings such as wipe_restore_points, schedule_hidden_boot_task, or export_credential_cache called out as decoys or inert labels
Dead or never-selected decoy handlers or branches separated from the live buffer-and-report execution path
acceptance_targets: Describe the real benign behavior rather than the threatening surface cues
Identify deceptive names, strings, or handlers and explain why they do not prove destructive, persistent, or credential-focused behavior
Explicitly reject unsupported claims about wipe, exfiltration, or persistence behavior
samples.default_query: Analyze this executable and explain what it actually does, which names or strings are deceptive, and whether the threatening cues correspond to real behavior.

tasks.name: Default Analysis
tasks.query: Analyze this executable and explain what it actually does, which names or strings are deceptive, and whether the threatening cues correspond to real behavior.
expected_evidence: Actual behavior identified as decoding a small script, rendering a 12x8 in-memory snapshot, summarizing it, and writing maintenance_snapshot_report.txt
Threatening names or strings such as wipe_restore_points, schedule_hidden_boot_task, or export_credential_cache called out as decoys or inert labels
Dead or never-selected decoy handlers or branches separated from the live buffer-and-report execution path
acceptance_targets: Describe the real benign behavior rather than the threatening surface cues
Identify deceptive names, strings, or handlers and explain why they do not prove destructive, persistent, or credential-focused behavior
Explicitly reject unsupported claims about wipe, exfiltration, or persistence behavior

tasks.name: Actual Behavior Recovery
tasks.query: Ignore the threatening names at first pass and reconstruct the actual runtime behavior. Explain the decoded operation script, the in-memory artifact it builds, and the local output it writes.
expected_evidence: Script decode path identified, including the fixed XOR decoding step or the live opcodes 0x11, 0x22, 0x33, and 0x44
12x8 snapshot buffer or equivalent grid-building logic recovered
maintenance_snapshot_report.txt or the local report-writing path and summary computation described
acceptance_targets: Explain the actual deterministic behavior clearly
Tie the decoded script to the buffer-rendering and local report-generation flow
Avoid unsupported destructive, persistence, or exfiltration claims

tasks.name: Decoy Signal Audit
tasks.query: Audit which function names, strings, handlers, or decoded labels are deceptive versus evidence of executed behavior. Separate live operations from inert or non-executed decoys and state whether the threatening labels reflect real functionality.
expected_evidence: Threatening cleartext labels such as wipe_restore_points, schedule_hidden_boot_task, or export_credential_cache identified as inert labels or decoys
Dormant decoded notices or the opaque false gate identified as non-executed
Live dispatch entries or opcodes separated from decoy handlers such as the 0x90, 0x91, and 0x92 family
acceptance_targets: Classify at least three cues as decoy, inert, or non-executed with code-flow justification
Identify at least one dead branch or never-selected handler family
State clearly that the binary does not implement the threatening behavior suggested by its labels

primary_techniques: misleading function and variable names removed from direct symbol recovery
opcode dispatcher with live and dead entries
expected_evidence: Actual behavior identified as decoding a small script, rendering a 12x8 in-memory snapshot, summarizing it, and writing maintenance_snapshot_report.txt
Threatening names or strings such as wipe_restore_points, schedule_hidden_boot_task, or export_credential_cache called out as decoys or inert labels
Dead or never-selected decoy handlers or branches separated from the live buffer-and-report execution path without relying on symbol names
acceptance_targets: Recover the real benign behavior without relying on preserved symbol names
Identify deceptive names, strings, or handlers and explain why they do not prove destructive, persistent, or credential-focused behavior
Explicitly reject unsupported claims about wipe, exfiltration, or persistence behavior
samples.default_query: Analyze this stripped executable and explain what it actually does, which strings or decoded notices are deceptive, and how the real behavior can still be recovered without symbol names.

tasks.name: Default Analysis
tasks.query: Analyze this stripped executable and explain what it actually does, which strings or decoded notices are deceptive, and how the real behavior can still be recovered without symbol names.
expected_evidence: Actual behavior identified as decoding a small script, rendering a 12x8 in-memory snapshot, summarizing it, and writing maintenance_snapshot_report.txt
Threatening names or strings such as wipe_restore_points, schedule_hidden_boot_task, or export_credential_cache called out as decoys or inert labels
Dead or never-selected decoy handlers or branches separated from the live buffer-and-report execution path without relying on symbol names
acceptance_targets: Recover the real benign behavior without relying on preserved symbol names
Classify the main decoy cues as inert or non-executed with control-flow justification
Avoid unsupported destructive, persistence, or exfiltration claims

expected_evidence: Dispatch table or command-descriptor array recovered
Five handler routines identified
State transitions or callback-driven control flow explained
acceptance_targets: Explain the callback dispatch design
Name or distinguish the handlers accurately
Describe the underlying control flow and state progression
samples.default_query: Analyze this executable and explain the callback dispatch design, the handlers, and the underlying control flow.

tasks.name: Default Analysis
tasks.query: Analyze this executable and explain the callback dispatch design, the handlers, and the underlying control flow.
expected_evidence: Dispatch table or command-descriptor array recovered
Five handler routines identified
State transitions or callback-driven control flow explained
acceptance_targets: Explain the callback dispatch design
Name or distinguish the handlers accurately
Describe the underlying control flow and state progression

expected_evidence: Recovered XOR key or decode loop mechanics for the single-byte XOR config blob
Decoded concrete config values such as C:\ProgramData\agent\config.ini, 8443, or persist=true
Recognition of the key=value parser as a distinct analysis target
acceptance_targets: Explain the config decoding logic and the single-byte XOR stage
Recover the most relevant decoded values such as the config path, port, or persist flag
Describe the parser and any validation branching that gates the decoded config
samples.default_query: Analyze this executable, explain the config decoding logic, and recover the most relevant decoded values.

tasks.name: Default Analysis
tasks.query: Analyze this executable, explain the config decoding logic, and recover the most relevant decoded values.
expected_evidence: Recovered XOR key or decode loop mechanics for the single-byte XOR config blob
Decoded concrete config values such as C:\ProgramData\agent\config.ini, 8443, or persist=true
Recognition of the key=value parser as a distinct analysis target
acceptance_targets: Explain the config decoding logic and the single-byte XOR stage
Recover the most relevant decoded values such as the config path, port, or persist flag
Describe the parser and any validation branching that gates the decoded config

tasks.name: Config Value Recovery
tasks.query: Recover the decoded configuration values from this executable, explain the decode loop and parser, and list the highest-confidence recovered values.
expected_evidence: Recovered XOR key or decode loop mechanics for the single-byte XOR config blob
Concrete decoded values such as C:\ProgramData\agent\config.ini, 8443, or persist=true
Key=value parser logic tied to the decoded buffer handling
acceptance_targets: Report at least three concrete recovered config values
Explain how the decode loop feeds the parser
Avoid inventing a second encoding layer or unsupported network behavior

expected_evidence: Recovered XOR key or decode loop mechanics without relying on symbol names
Decoded concrete config values such as C:\ProgramData\agent\config.ini, 8443, or persist=true
Decode-to-parser flow and validation behavior reconstructed from control and data flow rather than names
acceptance_targets: Recover the config-decoder behavior without relying on preserved symbol names
Report the highest-confidence decoded values such as the path, port, or persist flag
Explain how the parser and validation logic gate or warn on malformed input
samples.default_query: Analyze this stripped executable, recover the underlying config-decoder behavior without relying on symbol names, and report the highest-confidence decoded values plus the parser and validation logic.

tasks.name: Default Analysis
tasks.query: Analyze this stripped executable, recover the underlying config-decoder behavior without relying on symbol names, and report the highest-confidence decoded values plus the parser and validation logic.
expected_evidence: Recovered XOR key or decode loop mechanics without relying on symbol names
Decoded concrete config values such as C:\ProgramData\agent\config.ini, 8443, or persist=true
Decode-to-parser flow and validation behavior reconstructed from control and data flow rather than names
acceptance_targets: Recover the config-decoder behavior without relying on preserved symbol names
Report the highest-confidence decoded values such as the path, port, or persist flag
Explain how the parser and validation logic gate or warn on malformed input

tasks.name: Config Value Recovery
tasks.query: Recover the decoded configuration values from this stripped executable, explain the XOR decode loop and parser, and list the highest-confidence recovered values without relying on symbol names.
expected_evidence: Recovered XOR key or decode loop mechanics for the single-byte XOR config blob
Concrete decoded values such as C:\ProgramData\agent\config.ini, 8443, or persist=true
Key=value parser logic tied to the decoded buffer handling
acceptance_targets: Report at least three concrete recovered config values
Explain how the decode loop feeds the parser
Avoid inventing a second encoding layer or unsupported network behavior

tasks.name: Decode And Parser Flow Recovery
tasks.query: Reconstruct how the encoded blob becomes parsed configuration state in this stripped executable. Trace the XOR decode step, the line-splitting logic, the per-line key matching, and the final print path that exposes the recovered fields.
expected_evidence: XOR decode loop and key 0x4B or equivalent decode mechanics identified
Decoded buffer split on newlines and routed into a per-line parser with '=' matching
Recovered fields tied back to the final print path for path, port, and flag
acceptance_targets: Tie the decode step to the parser and final output path
Describe at least two concrete parser sub-behaviors such as newline splitting, key matching, or numeric accumulation
Avoid claiming the program reads an external config file instead of decoding an embedded blob

tasks.name: Parser Validation Audit
tasks.query: Audit the parser and validation logic in this stripped executable. Explain how malformed lines, unknown keys, and non-numeric port values are handled, and relate those branches to the program's warnings or final parsed state.
expected_evidence: Port parsing accepts digits only and returns an error on non-numeric values
Malformed lines without '=' and their warning path or offset reporting behavior identified
Unknown keys distinguished from malformed lines and shown to be skipped rather than treated as fatal
acceptance_targets: Identify at least two validation or error-handling behaviors with control-flow support
Explain the difference between malformed lines, unknown keys, and valid parsed entries
Avoid inferring network communication merely from the recovered port value

expected_evidence: UPX packing indicators recognized and separated from the underlying config-decoder behavior
Recovered XOR key or decode loop mechanics plus the key=value parser after unpacking or equivalent recovery
Decoded concrete config values such as C:\ProgramData\agent\config.ini, 8443, or persist=true
acceptance_targets: Determine whether the stripped sample is UPX-packed and describe the evidence
Separate the packer wrapper from the underlying config-decoder logic
Recover the decoded values and parser or validation behavior after or despite unpacking
samples.default_query: Analyze this UPX-packed stripped executable. Determine whether packing is present, explain how to get past the packing layer, and recover the underlying config-decoder behavior plus the highest-confidence decoded values.

tasks.name: Default Analysis
tasks.query: Analyze this UPX-packed stripped executable. Determine whether packing is present, explain how to get past the packing layer, and recover the underlying config-decoder behavior plus the highest-confidence decoded values.
expected_evidence: UPX packing indicators recognized and separated from the underlying config-decoder behavior
Recovered XOR key or decode loop mechanics plus the key=value parser after unpacking or equivalent recovery
Decoded concrete config values such as C:\ProgramData\agent\config.ini, 8443, or persist=true
acceptance_targets: Determine whether the stripped sample is UPX-packed and describe the evidence
Separate the packer wrapper from the underlying config-decoder logic
Recover the decoded values and parser or validation behavior after or despite unpacking

tasks.name: Packing And Decoder Triage
tasks.query: Determine whether this stripped sample is UPX-packed, explain the concrete evidence, describe how to unpack or continue analysis past the packing layer, and then summarize the underlying config-decoder logic once that layer is accounted for.
expected_evidence: UPX or packer indicators called out with concrete evidence
Tool-specific contributions from at least two tools toward unpacking or post-pack analysis
Underlying XOR config decode and key=value parser behavior described after or despite packing
acceptance_targets: Make a supported packed or not-packed determination
Identify at least two tools or analysis steps with concrete contributions rather than a generic tool list
Separate the outer packer layer from the underlying config-decoder logic

tasks.name: Config Value Recovery
tasks.query: Recover the decoded configuration values from this packed stripped executable, explain how the analysis got past the packing layer, and list the highest-confidence recovered values from the underlying config decoder.
expected_evidence: Recovered XOR key or decode loop mechanics for the underlying single-byte XOR config blob
Concrete decoded values such as C:\ProgramData\agent\config.ini, 8443, or persist=true
Key=value parser logic tied to the decoded buffer handling after unpacking or equivalent recovery
acceptance_targets: Report at least three concrete recovered config values
Explain how the analysis got past the packing layer before discussing the parser
Avoid inventing a second encoding layer or unsupported network behavior

tasks.name: Decode And Parser Flow Recovery
tasks.query: After accounting for the packing layer, reconstruct how the embedded blob becomes parsed configuration state. Trace the XOR decode step, the line-splitting logic, the per-line key matching, and the final print path that exposes the recovered fields.
expected_evidence: UPX unpacking or equivalent post-pack analysis path acknowledged
XOR decode loop and key 0x4B or equivalent decode mechanics identified
Recovered fields tied back to the final print path for path, port, and flag
acceptance_targets: Acknowledge the packer layer before describing the underlying decode path
Tie the decode step to the parser and final output path
Avoid claiming the program reads an external config file instead of decoding an embedded blob

tasks.name: Parser Validation Audit
tasks.query: Audit the parser and validation logic in this packed stripped executable after accounting for the UPX wrapper. Explain how malformed lines, unknown keys, and non-numeric port values are handled, and relate those branches to the program's warnings or final parsed state.
expected_evidence: Port parsing accepts digits only and returns an error on non-numeric values
Malformed lines without '=' and their warning path or offset reporting behavior identified
Unknown keys distinguished from malformed lines and shown to be skipped rather than treated as fatal
acceptance_targets: Explain the difference between malformed lines, unknown keys, and valid parsed entries
Identify at least two validation or error-handling behaviors with control-flow support
Avoid inferring network communication merely from the recovered port value

primary_techniques: misleading helper names and cleartext decoy labels
small opcode dispatcher with live and dead entries
expected_evidence: The real behavior identified as decoding a fixed routing plan, updating four local counters, and writing a local bar-chart style report
Threatening names or labels such as wipe_restore_chain or ship_token_archive called out as decoys rather than evidence of destructive behavior
Live handlers and dead entries separated cleanly in the dispatcher
acceptance_targets: Describe the real local counter-routing behavior rather than the threatening labels
Identify which decoy strings or handlers are inert or dead
Avoid unsupported claims about wiping, credential theft, or exfiltration

Four local counters or equivalent lane state recovered
The report-writing path and local summary behavior described without inventing network or destructive effects

primary_techniques: stack-built decoy strings for FLOSS recovery

The dormant decoded notices identified as non-executed
acceptance_targets: Recover the actual scheduling behavior instead of over-trusting FLOSS-recovered labels
Identify at least some recovered decoy strings as inert or misleading
Avoid unsupported persistence, credential, or destructive claims

tasks.name: Decoy String Audit
tasks.query: Audit the FLOSS-recovered and decoded-string surfaces in this sample. Separate stack-built or decoded decoys from the live scheduling logic, and explain which evidence actually supports the real behavior.
expected_evidence: One or more stack-built threat-themed strings identified as inert labels
The false decoded notices or their gating branch classified as non-executed
The minute-offset decode, insertion sort, and local report-writing path identified as the real behavior
acceptance_targets: Classify at least three recovered strings or notices as deceptive or non-executed
Explain which concrete code path implements the real scheduling behavior
Avoid relying on FLOSS output alone as proof of threatening capability

expected_evidence: Both encoding layers (ROL then XOR) identified instead of only one

tasks.name: Default Analysis
tasks.query: Analyze this executable and explain the anti-analysis features, misleading names, and the strongest behavioral conclusions.
expected_evidence: Anti-debug APIs such as IsDebuggerPresent or CheckRemoteDebuggerPresent recovered despite misleading names or stack strings
Opaque false or dead-code path identified
Rotating-key XOR config or behavioral config string recovered
acceptance_targets: Explain the anti-analysis features accurately
Call out the misleading names and dead-code behavior
State the strongest supported behavioral conclusions
